channel.title: NYT > U.S. > Politics
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Immigrant Arrests Surge to 10,000 in 5 Days as ICE Clamps Down
item.description: The agency has doubled its daily arrest numbers without the fanfare of last year’s large urban operations, sowing fear in immigrant communities.
item.dc:creator: Hamed Aleaziz
item.media:credit: Michael M. Santiago/Getty Images
item.media:description: Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents patrolling a federal court building in New York last month.

item.title: How Trump Made ‘Y.M.C.A.’ His Anthem, Despite the Village People and Victor Willis’s Mixed Feelings
item.description: On social media, he said the Village People’s lead singer, Victor Willis, was with him “right from the beginning.” But the president has a more complicated history with the group.
item.dc:creator: Aishvarya Kavi
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: President-elect Donald J. Trump danced to “Y.M.C.A.” along with the Village People during a pre-inauguration event in January last year.

item.title: Bureau of Prisons Will Close Facilities Housing Thousands of Inmates
item.description: The agency pointed to crumbling infrastructure, chronic staffing shortages and budget shortfalls as it moved to cut costs.
item.dc:creator: Chris Cameron
item.media:credit: Julia Robinson for The New York Times
item.media:description: The federal prison in Big Spring, Texas, is among the facilities to close.

item.title: Ruling on Trans Athletes Gave the G.O.P. a Win. Most Democrats Looked the Other Way.
item.description: While Republicans celebrated the ruling, many Democrats stayed quiet on an issue that had proved divisive in the last election.
item.dc:creator: Emily Davies and Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Republicans celebrated the Supreme Court’s ruling this week upholding laws banning transgender athletes from competing in women’s sports. Democrats were largely silent on the matter.

item.title: Trump Administration Delivers Lucrative Win for Its Kratom Allies
item.description: In moving to ban a potent synthetic version of kratom, the president’s team paved the way for more sales for makers of rival botanic supplements, who had aggressively lobbied for the change.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett and Kenneth P. Vogel
item.media:credit: Nick Oxford for The New York Times
item.media:description: After President Trump’s victory, companies selling kratom supplements and their executives ramped up their spending on lobbyists with ties to his administration and donations to his political operation.

item.title: Trump Finds His Presidential Alter Ego, the ‘He-Man’ Teddy Roosevelt
item.description: President Trump flew into town on the new Air Force One and spent time touring the library dedicated to Theodore Roosevelt, whom he called “a great he-man.”
item.dc:creator: Shawn McCreesh
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump arrived on a train at the Theodore Roosevelt Presidential Library in North Dakota on Wednesday.

item.title: Does Trump Worry About Conflicts of Interest? ‘I Found Out That Nobody Cared.’
item.description: American presidents have generally tried to avoid appearing to profit from the office. President Trump has chosen a different path.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump, followed by his sons Donald Jr. and Eric, steps off the new Air Force One in Bismarck, N.D., on Wednesday.

item.title: The Key Ways Trump’s Financial Interests Intersect With Government Policy
item.description: President Trump’s business holdings, which garnered him more than $2 billion last year, create potential conflicts of interest surpassing any predecessor.
item.dc:creator: Ben Protess, Andrea Fuller and Eric Lipton
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump’s financial investments have vastly escalated during this second term.

item.title: America Rings In 250th Birthday Amid Deep Polarization and Political Rifts
item.description: How does a deeply polarized country come together to have a birthday party?
item.dc:creator: Patricia Mazzei
item.media:credit: Alex Kent/The New York Times
item.media:description: The festivities at the Great American State Fair on the National Mall.

item.title: Judge Blocks Postal Service From Imposing Restrictions on Mail-In Ballots
item.description: The ruling, based on agreements the service made in a 2021 legal settlement, was the second time a judge has blocked new curbs by President Trump on voting by mail.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague and Adam Sella
item.category: National Assn for the Advancement of Colored People
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mail-in ballots being sorted in City of Industry, Calif., last year.

item.title: Promising Much to Many, Johnson Loses His Grip on the House
item.description: Republicans are growing frustrated with Mr. Johnson’s approach to governing with a razor-thin majority, saying that he promises more than he can achieve, frustrating the disparate groups in his caucus.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold and Carl Hulse
item.media:credit: Demetrius Freeman for The New York Times

item.title: Crypto Brought Trump a Huge Windfall, Even as Many Investors Lost Big
item.description: President Trump and his family reaped vast financial rewards from a memecoin that generated losses for hundreds of thousands of investors.
item.dc:creator: Eric Lipton, Andrea Fuller and David Yaffe-Bellany
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump amassed enormous wealth during the first year of his second term, with $636 million of it coming from his cryptocurrency.

item.title: Minnesota Pardons Sexual Abuser Who Was Set to Be Deported
item.description: Tou Lue Vang has expressed regret for abusing a 10-year-old two decades ago. The Trump administration accused Gov. Tim Walz, a Democrat, of shielding an immigrant who committed a serious crime.
item.dc:creator: Hamed Aleaziz, Ernesto Londoño and Amy Qin
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gov. Tim Walz of Minnesota has been a frequent target of the Trump White House, especially over immigration.

item.title: How JD Vance Helped bell hooks Back Onto the Best-Seller List
item.description: A grass-roots campaign urged readers to buy copies of the Black feminist writer’s 2002 book “Communion” before Vice President JD Vance’s book of the same name hit shelves.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter
item.category: Communion: Finding My Way Back to Faith (Book)
Hillbilly Elegy: A Memoir of a Family and Culture in Crisis (Book)
Communion: The Female Search for Love (Book)
item.media:credit: David Degner for The New York Times

item.title: Trump Administration Sues Over New Gun Bans in Virginia and California
item.description: The Justice Department is challenging a ban on semiautomatic assault rifles in Virginia and a ban on Glocks and Glock-style pistols in California, arguing they are unlawful under the Second Amendment.
item.dc:creator: Aishvarya Kavi
item.media:credit: Bing Guan/Reuters
item.media:description: Glock semiautomatic pistols were displayed at Firearms Unknown, a gun store in Oceanside, Calif., in 2021.

item.title: The Democratic Incumbents Most at Risk of Losing to Progressive Primary Challengers in 2026
item.description: A wave of outsider fervor has already knocked out a number of incumbents in primary races. Several more face spirited opposition.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Rebecca Slezak/Associated Press
item.media:description: Melat Kiros, a 29-year-old democratic socialist, defeated Representative Diana DeGette of Colorado, a 68-year-old incumbent running for a 16th term on Tuesday.

item.title: Trump Remakes Washington, D.C., Into a Maze of Fences and Guard Members
item.description: President Trump seems to have turned swaths of the city into either a construction zone or an armed camp as he seeks to prove that he alone can improve it.
item.dc:creator: Katie Rogers
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times
item.media:description: Federal officials have effectively closed off access to the Reflecting Pool ahead of the nation’s 250th anniversary.

item.title: Ex-C.I.A. Chief Asks for Order Forcing Trump Administration to Preserve Records
item.description: Lawyers for John O. Brennan said they want the materials saved in anticipation of bringing a vindictive prosecution motion if charges against Mr. Brennan are ultimately filed.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer
item.category: Russian Interference in 2016 US Elections and Ties to Trump Associates
Federal Criminal Case Against Trump (Documents Case)
item.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times
item.media:description: Almost everything about the inquiries into John O. Brennan, the former C.I.A. director, has been unorthodox.

item.title: Troop Casualties in Ukraine War Top 2 Million, Study Finds
item.description: Russia has borne the heavier toll, with 1.4 million troops killed or wounded since it invaded in February 2022.
item.dc:creator: Helene Cooper
item.media:credit: Nicole Tung for The New York Times
item.media:description: A funeral for a Ukrainian soldier in Kyiv last year. Ukrainian forces have suffered 525,000 to 625,000 casualties, the study said.

item.title: Trump Takes First Flight on New Air Force One From Qatar
item.description: The Boeing 747-8, a gift worth $200 million from the Qatari royal family, has attracted considerable scrutiny over whether a foreign government was trying to influence the president.
item.dc:creator: Karoun Demirjian
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times

item.title: How the Iran War Ignited a Clash Between Trump and the Saudi Crown Prince
item.description: Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman pressed President Trump earlier to cripple Iran. But as Iran asserted its power, the prince urged a cease-fire, and is now pursuing his security priorities.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump with Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman of Saudi Arabia in Washington last year. The two countries have become increasingly at odds over the U.S. war with Iran.

item.title: Republicans’ Home-Field Edge for Senate Seems Just Enough for Now
item.description: Democratic candidates are generally popular, Times/Siena polling finds, but retaking the Senate remains a big challenge.
item.dc:creator: Nate Cohn
item.media:credit: Nathan Howard/Reuters
item.media:description: Representative Ashley Hinson, a Republican, has a narrow lead in the Senate race in Iowa.

item.title: The Birthright Decision Was Surprisingly Close, Some Legal Scholars Say
item.description: A bare majority of Supreme Court justices ruled that President Trump’s executive order was unconstitutional, reflecting a conservative shift on the issue.
item.dc:creator: Amy Qin
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert for The New York Times
item.media:description: Birthright citizenship as a constitutional right survived by one vote.

item.title: Control of the Senate Is Up for Grabs, Times/Siena Polls Find
item.description: Republicans are defending seats in Alaska, Iowa, Maine, North Carolina, Ohio and Texas as they try to maintain their majority. Democrats are competitive in all six states — but not leading in enough to take the chamber.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher, Ruth Igielnik and Camille Baker
item.media:credit: Edison Wu/The New York Times

item.title: Fact-Checking Trump’s Inaccurate Accounts of U.S. History
item.description: We fact-checked the president’s colorful — and inaccurate — accounts of American history from the Battle of Gettysburg to the Unabomber.
item.dc:creator: Linda Qiu and Jamie Leventhal
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: Since retaking office, President Trump has spun many historical yarns that stretch the truth, strain credulity or are downright false.

item.title: Despite Some Losses for Trump, Supreme Court Delivers Enduring Conservative Wins
item.description: The justices pushed back on some of President Trump’s signature moves, but they also expanded presidential power and supplied victories on long-sought conservative goals.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow and Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Supreme Court struck down President Trump’s order to end the guarantee of birthright citizenship for the U.S.-born babies of undocumented immigrants and temporary visitors.
